title: AI Weekly Tips — One Idea per Week
subtitle: A 34-week rotation of one small AI move you can try this week. Designed for the Eduversal Indonesian Cambridge academic calendar.

audience._note: Tips are written for classroom teachers because that's the largest audience. Each tip has an 'alsoUsefulFor' field showing when leaders or specialists can use the same idea.

_meta.purpose: Open this page on Monday morning. Read one tip. Try it that week. Close the page. Come back next Monday. The library exists because reading the full playbook once is hard; reading one paragraph per week is easy.
_meta.writingNote: Plain English. One idea per tip. One concrete action. No reading homework.

academicYearShape.rationale: Eduversal Indonesian Cambridge academic year has 34 effective teaching weeks. Tips are anchored to the rhythm of the year — start-of-year, mid-term checks, exam windows, report writing, end-of-year — so the right tip lands at the right time.
academicYearShape.semestersOverview: Semester 1: Weeks 1-17. Semester 2: Weeks 18-34. Exact dates vary by school.

weeklyChecklistIntegration.parallel_not_merged: Eduversal already has a weekly-checklist system (8 role JSONs at docs/weekly-checklists/, seeding weekly_templates/{academicYear}_w{NN}_{platform} Firestore docs). This file is PARALLEL to that system — same week numbering rhythm, but a separate file for separate purpose. AI tips do NOT live inside the weekly-checklist Firestore docs.
weeklyChecklistIntegration.crossLinkPattern: When an AI tip naturally pairs with an existing weekly-checklist task, the tip carries a `pairedTaskIds` field with task IDs from the weekly-checklist (TT-* for subject teachers, SL-* for subject leaders, SP-* for school principals, AC-* for academic coordinators, CC-* for Cambridge coordinators, FR-* for foundation reps, SS-* for HQ subject specialists, DR-* for directors).

howToUseThisRotation.format: Each week has ONE tip. Read it Monday. Try the suggested action by Friday. That's the whole protocol.
howToUseThisRotation.ifYouMissAWeek: Don't try to catch up. Skip to the current week. The rotation is designed so missing a week doesn't break anything.
howToUseThisRotation.ifYouAreNewMidYear: Start at the current week, not Week 1. Tips work in any order — they don't build on each other.
howToUseThisRotation.ifYouAreLeaderOrSpecialist: Read the same tips as teachers. The 'alsoUsefulFor' field tells you how to adapt the idea for your role.
howToUseThisRotation.afterTheYear: The rotation repeats next year, but content gets refreshed each year (see versionHistory). Tips that became outdated are replaced; tips that worked stay.

weeks.title: Bookmark this page
weeks.tip: Bookmark this page in your browser. The whole AI guide system has eight files; this is the one you'll open most often. Look at the next 3-4 weeks of tips so you know what's coming.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Bookmark this page. Read the next 3 tips so you have a sense of the pacing.

weeks.title: Read the red lines once
weeks.tip: Before you use AI for anything school-related, know what you must never do. The 10 red lines fit on one page. Skim them. You don't have to memorise — just know they exist.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Open redFlagsAndRedlines.json. Read the 10 short rules. Note any that surprise you.

weeks.title: Pick your one main tool
weeks.tip: Use only the tool your school has approved. Don't switch tools every week. Pick one, learn it well. The differences between the core three (Claude for Education / ChatGPT for Teachers / Gemini for Education) matter less than getting fluent in one.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Confirm with your Academic Coordinator which AI tool is school-approved. Log in. Set it as a bookmark. Use only this one for school work this term.

weeks.title: Your first prompt — lesson plan skeleton
weeks.tip: Pick one lesson you're planning this week. Use the lesson plan skeleton prompt from the prompt library. Spend the time it saves on the part only you can do — anticipating where your students will get stuck.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Open prompt-library.json#lessonPlanning.skeletonDraft. Copy. Fill in the placeholders for one of your lessons this week. Try it.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: If your weekly-checklist requires lesson plans submitted by Friday (TT-C1-001), use this prompt to accelerate the drafting. Plans still need YOUR review and your specific class context before submission.

weeks.title: Anonymise everything
weeks.tip: Practice this habit until it's automatic: never type a student's name. Say 'a Year 8 student' or 'a class with 3 EAL students' instead. UU PDP No. 27/2022 makes this a real compliance issue, not just a preference.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Catch yourself once this week before pasting student data. Replace names with descriptions. Notice if the AI's output is just as useful (it usually is).

weeks.title: Three versions instead of one
weeks.tip: You probably already make one practice set for the on-level student. Differentiation is the #1 AI use case in 2025-2026 because the time saving is huge. Try generating access + stretch versions of one practice set this week.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Pick one practice set you already use. Use the differentiation.threeVersions prompt. Compare which version which students need.
weeks.provenance: practitioner-pattern (Anthropic Education Report 2025: 61% of teacher AI use is differentiation)

weeks.title: Iterate, don't one-shot
weeks.tip: First-turn output is rarely the best answer. Practitioner-level AI use is conversation, not query. After the first response, ask: 'Where is this weak?' or 'Give me three alternatives.' or 'What's missing?'
weeks.thisWeekAction: Pick one AI conversation this week. After the first response, ask at least 2 follow-up questions before using the output. Notice the difference.

weeks.title: Subject-specific notes — read your section
weeks.tip: AI behaves differently in different subjects. Maths AI is strong at practice generation, weak at deep concept scaffolding. English AI is strong at vocabulary, weak at marking voice. Read the notes for your subject.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Open teacher-playbook.json#coreWorkflows.lessonPlanning.subjectSpecificNotes. Read only your subject's paragraph.

weeks.title: First rubric draft with AI
weeks.tip: If you're setting a new task this week that you'll mark formatively, try drafting the rubric with AI. Four bands (Emerging / Developing / Secure / Exceeding), three criteria. Edit the descriptors before using.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Pick one upcoming task. Use the rubric.fourBandDraft prompt. Test the rubric on one piece of imagined exemplar work before using it.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: Assessment and feedback tasks (Category C3) benefit from rubric work. AI drafts the rubric; you moderate it with your department before using.

weeks.title: Catch one AI error this week
weeks.tip: AI makes confident-sounding mistakes. Cultural-bias errors. Made-up Indonesian facts. Wrong Cambridge AO citations. Spotting these is the difference between Foundation and Practitioner level. This week, notice and fix one AI error before using the output.
weeks.thisWeekAction: When using AI this week, deliberately look for one error in the output. Write down what kind it was. Keep noticing.

weeks.title: Mid-term reset — what's working?
weeks.tip: About a third of the way through the year. Time for a small reflection. Which AI workflow has saved you the most time? Which one did you try and abandon? You'll keep what works for the rest of the year.
weeks.thisWeekAction: 5 minutes of reflection. Write down your top 1-2 AI workflows. Drop anything you're not using.

weeks.title: First parent message draft with AI
weeks.tip: If you have a parent message to send this week — concern, recognition, anything — try drafting with AI. Use the bilingual prompt if needed. Edit at least 30% in your own voice before sending. Generic AI tone is detected fast.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Use prompt-library.json#parentCommunication.bilingualDraft for one parent message. Edit substantially. Send.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: Category C6 (Stakeholder Engagement) tasks often involve parent contact. AI drafts the message; your voice and your specific knowledge of the family/student make the final version.

weeks.title: Hinge questions for one lesson
weeks.tip: Hinge questions are 30-second mid-lesson checks that tell you whether to move on or re-teach. AI is good at drafting these once you give it the misconception you want to surface. Try it on one lesson this week.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Pick one lesson this week. Use prompt-library.json#feedback.hingeQuestions. Drop the questions into the lesson at the natural decision point.

weeks.title: What AI should NOT do
weeks.tip: Exams are coming. This is the time of year when shortcuts feel tempting. Reminder: AI never grades real work. AI never makes decisions about real students. AI never authors final-form assessment items. Re-read red lines 2 and 3 if you need to.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Read redFlagsAndRedlines.json#rl_02 and #rl_03. 2 minutes total. Confirm you're not drifting.

weeks.title: Process-based integrity, not detection
weeks.tip: If you're worried about students using AI on coursework, the answer is NOT AI detection tools. They are unreliable for EAL writers — false-positive rates are high enough that major universities disabled Turnitin AI detection in 2025. Use process-based integrity instead: in-class drafts, version history, oral defence.
weeks.thisWeekAction: For one piece of work students will submit this week, plan a 3-minute oral check after submission. 'Walk me through how you did this.' That's the integrity check.
weeks.provenance: practitioner-pattern (2023 Patterns study + Anthropic + OECD 2025 recommendations)

weeks.title: Don't grade with AI
weeks.tip: When the stack of exam papers is high, AI grading looks tempting. Don't. AI can draft feedback templates that you adapt. AI cannot mark. This is red line 2 and the firmest line in the playbook.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Use prompt-library.json#feedback.commentBankFromRubric to make feedback templates BEFORE you mark. Adapt the templates per student as you mark.

weeks.title: Report-writing efficiency
weeks.tip: Report writing is grinding. AI helps a lot — but only as a comment-bank generator that you adapt per student. Never paste student names into a public AI tool. Never use AI scores as the basis for the comment.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Build one comment template per rubric band before you write reports. Adapt for each student. Editing a template is faster than writing from scratch AND more personal than generic AI output.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: Mid-year reports land around Week 17. Comment templates built once, adapted per student, beat both blank-slate writing AND unedited AI output.
weeks.provenance: practitioner-pattern (Anthropic Education Report 2025: report card writing is a top educator use case)

weeks.title: Fresh start — what you'll keep, what you'll drop
weeks.tip: New semester. Look at what AI workflows you used in Semester 1 and decide what stays. Don't add new workflows yet — first stabilise what already works.
weeks.thisWeekAction: 5 minutes. Write down the 2-3 AI workflows you'll use weekly in Semester 2. Stop everything else.

weeks.title: Try one new prompt this term
weeks.tip: Now that your core workflows are stable, expand. Look at the prompt library. Pick one prompt you haven't used yet. Try it this week.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Open prompt-library.json. Scan the categories. Pick one prompt that isn't in your current weekly rotation. Use it on one task this week.

weeks.title: EAL rephrasing — for one student
weeks.tip: If you have EAL students in any of your classes, the EAL rephrasing prompt is one of the highest-impact AI uses. Same cognitive demand, lower language load. Try it on one complex task this week.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Use prompt-library.json#differentiation.ealRephrase on one task. Share the rephrased version with your EAL students. Notice the engagement difference.

weeks.title: Student-facing AI literacy lesson
weeks.tip: Once per term per class: run a 'critique an AI output' lesson. Students annotate an AI response in your subject. This single lesson does more for AI literacy than a dozen 'AI safety' assemblies. Now is a good time to plan one.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Use prompt-library.json#studentActivity.critiqueAnAiOutput to prepare the lesson. Run it within the next 2-3 weeks.

weeks.title: Misconception-targeted practice
weeks.tip: Pick a topic where students reliably get one specific thing wrong. Use AI to draft practice problems that trap that specific misconception. Higher pedagogical value than generic practice, and AI removes the time barrier.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Use prompt-library.json#differentiation.misconceptionAware on one topic where you know the typical Year [N] misconception.

weeks.title: Walk through your department's AI use
weeks.tip: If you lead a subject department, walk through this week and ask one passing question to each department member: 'What's an AI move that worked this week?' Builds department culture without making AI feel surveilled.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Have the conversation with at least 3 department members this week. Note common patterns.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: Subject Leader walkthroughs (Category C2 — Instructional Practice) and PD conversations (C4) naturally cover AI use. Add one passing AI question to the conversations you're already having.

weeks.title: Iterate on a prompt that didn't work
weeks.tip: Most 'AI didn't work' situations are 'my prompt was too short'. Pick one task where AI gave you bad output recently. Add more context. Try again. The Practitioner-level skill is iteration.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Find a prompt you gave up on. Add: role, context, year group, syllabus reference, constraints. Try again. Notice the difference.

weeks.title: What's your school's AI policy?
weeks.tip: Do you actually know what your school's AI policy says? Many teachers don't. Find it. Read it. If your school doesn't have one yet, raise it to your Academic Coordinator.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Locate your school's AI policy this week. Read it. Note any rules that surprise you. If there's no policy, raise it.

weeks.title: AI doesn't see your students
weeks.tip: As Term 2 progresses, you may find yourself relying on AI for things you used to think about yourself. Reset moment: AI doesn't know your students. Decisions about real children come from you, your colleagues, and the family — not from a chat window.
weeks.thisWeekAction: If you've drifted toward AI for student-specific decisions, pull back. Re-read redFlagsAndRedlines.json#rl_05.

weeks.title: Hinge questions for revision lessons
weeks.tip: Revision lessons benefit from many quick check-questions. AI is good at drafting these once you give it the topic and the year group. Use them to find out what's actually unclear, not to confirm what you hope is clear.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Draft hinge questions for one revision lesson. Use them mid-lesson. Adjust the rest of the lesson based on what students show.

weeks.title: Don't grade. Draft templates.
weeks.tip: Same reminder as Week 16. End-of-year marking pressure makes AI grading look attractive again. Don't. Templates yes; marking no. The red line doesn't change with the season.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Build comment-bank templates BEFORE you mark, not while you mark. Adapt per student during marking.

weeks.title: Report writing — bilingual where needed
weeks.tip: If your school sends reports in both English and Bahasa Indonesia, the bilingual workflow is your friend. Draft in English. Use DeepL Pro or Claude for Bahasa. Read the Bahasa version aloud before sending — register matters.
weeks.thisWeekAction: For one bilingual report this week, use the bilingual draft prompt + Bahasa verification pass.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: End-of-year reports combine assessment work (C3) with parent communication (C6). Build templates once, adapt per student, translate for Bahasa parents.

weeks.title: Year-end audit — what did AI actually do?
weeks.tip: Honest moment: what did AI actually save you this year? Not what you hoped. What you actually used. This shapes your self-assessment and your plan for next year.
weeks.thisWeekAction: 5 minutes. List 2-3 specific things AI saved you time on this year. List 1-2 things you tried and abandoned. Note this for your self-assessment.

weeks.title: Annual self-assessment — be honest
weeks.tip: Your annual AI competency self-assessment is honest progress, not performance theatre. If you're Foundation, mark Foundation. Inflated ratings get caught by your line manager and break trust in next year's process.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Open decision-trees.json#amIReadyForPractitionerLevel. Walk through the 5 questions honestly. Mark your level accordingly.
weeks.pairedTaskNote: Category C4 (Professional Development) tasks cover annual self-assessment cycles. The AI self-assessment is parallel to these — both honest and both writes-to-Firestore (ai_competency_self_assessments + existing weekly_progress).

weeks.title: Share one prompt with a colleague
weeks.tip: If you've found a prompt that works well this year, give it to a colleague. This is what moves you from Foundation to Practitioner. It's also how the prompt library grows — sustained contributions from teachers who tested things.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Pick one prompt you've used dozens of times. Share it with one colleague. If it's genuinely useful, suggest your Academic Coordinator forward it to HQ for the library.

weeks.title: Set one goal for next year
weeks.tip: Next year you'll either have stable AI workflows or you'll still be reinventing them weekly. The difference is one or two specific goals you set now.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Write one specific AI-related goal for next year. Examples: 'I'll use the differentiation workflow for every unit in my Year 8 maths classes.' / 'I'll run one student-facing AI literacy lesson per term.' / 'I'll move from Foundation to Practitioner level.'

weeks.title: Rest. The rotation starts again next year.
weeks.tip: Last week. You don't need a new AI move. You need rest. The rotation will start again next year, with refreshed content. The work you did this year stays with you.
weeks.thisWeekAction: Close the tab. Take the break. See you next academic year.

alternativeRotations._purpose: Not every educator starts the academic year at Week 1. Tips work in any order. Here are non-linear paths through the rotation if your situation calls for one.

newMidYear.title: If you're new to AI mid-year
newMidYear.description: Foundation tips first (1-5), then your first prompt (4), iteration habit (7), differentiation (6), error spotting (10), rubric (9), parent communication (12). Skip the year-rhythm-specific tips until they're seasonal again.

alreadyAtPractitionerLevel.title: If you're already Practitioner-level coming into the year
alreadyAtPractitionerLevel.description: Skip the Foundation refresher. Go straight to misconception-targeted practice (22), student AI literacy (21), expanding your prompt set (19), department-level work if you lead one (23), and end-of-year contribution + self-assessment (32, 30, 31, 33).

subjectLeaderSpecific.title: If you're a Subject Leader
subjectLeaderSpecific.description: Tool selection (3), subject-specific notes (8), mid-term reset (11), department walkthrough (23), year-end audit (30), peer sharing (32). Plus read all teacher tips as a participant.

schoolLeaderSpecific.title: If you're a school leader (Principal / Foundation Rep / Academic Coordinator)
schoolLeaderSpecific.description: Red lines (2), school policy review (25), what AI should NOT do (14), academic integrity approach (15), late-year marking discipline (28), self-assessment (31), goal-setting (33). Plus, additional weekly habits from leader-playbook.json.

academicYearPhases._purpose: Map of weeks to broader phases — useful when planning department meetings, training days, or policy reviews around the rhythm.

phases.phase: Exam Window + Reports — Red line vigilance
End of Year — Reports + Reflection + Goals

pairedDocuments.teacherPlaybook: teacher-playbook.json — full workflows that tips reference
pairedDocuments.leaderPlaybook: leader-playbook.json — when leader-relevant weeks come up
pairedDocuments.promptLibrary: prompt-library.json — most weeks reference a specific prompt
pairedDocuments.decisionTrees: decision-trees.json — referenced in iteration and self-assessment weeks

v1.0_2026-05-16.added: Initial 34-week rotation aligned to Eduversal Indonesian Cambridge academic year (approximately 34 effective teaching weeks across 2 semesters).
v1.0_2026-05-16.phaseDistribution: Foundation (weeks 1-7) → Confidence (8-11) → Expansion (12-13, 19-24) → Discipline + Red line vigilance (14-17, 25-28) → Reflection + Goals (29-34).
knownAbsences: No subject-specific weeks (every week works for all subjects with adaptation). Subject-specific tip threads may be added in v1.1.
No specialist-specific weeks — HQ specialists read teacher tips for their own teaching work; specialist-specific tip set deferred.
Religious / cultural calendar alignment (Ramadan timing varies year-to-year) — tips do not assume specific calendar dates.

versionHistory.annualReviewExpected: Each Term 3, replace tips that didn't land with new ones based on what worked. Keep tips that stayed useful.

contributionInvitation._purpose: Best tips come from teachers who tested things across an academic year.
howToContribute: If a tip didn't work for you, tell your Academic Coordinator what specifically didn't land.
If you developed your own weekly habit that worked across the year, write it up (1 paragraph + 1 concrete action + 1 reference) and forward.
Contributions reviewed at Term 3 each year for inclusion in next year's rotation.

items: Religious/cultural calendar alignment — Ramadan timing affects Semester 2 rhythm differently each year. Tips currently calendar-neutral; school-level adaptation expected.
Whether week 34 should be a teacher-only week or include leader/specialist tasks — currently teacher-focused. Open.
Subject-specific rotations — possible v1.1 addition. Open.
